critique.summary: The article discusses the recent challenges faced by Deno, including layoffs, a 404 error on their website, and concerns about the company's future and leadership visibility. It highlights criticisms of Deno's adoption rates, product issues, and mixed messaging, while also noting some positive aspects and hopes for the runtime's survival.
mainPoints: Deno's website showed a 404 error, symbolizing the company's current troubles.
Recent layoffs have affected a significant portion of Deno's staff, raising concerns about the company's stability.
Deno received $4.9M in seed funding and $21M in Series A funding but struggles with growth and developer adoption.
Ryan Dahl, Deno's CEO, acknowledged some valid criticisms and noted that adoption doubled after Deno 2.0's release.
Deno Deploy, the main revenue source, faced performance issues and limited user interest.
The Deno ecosystem projects like JSR struggled due to lack of user interest and infrastructure investment.
Deno made a U-turn on HTTP imports but created confusion with mixed messaging and documentation.
There is speculation about Deno pivoting towards AI and rumors of an OpenAI acquisition, though these are unconfirmed.
The article calls for clearer communication and leadership from Ryan Dahl regarding Deno's future.
critique.truthfulness: The article contains a mix of verifiable facts and subjective opinions. Funding amounts and timeline are consistent with publicly available data on Deno's funding history. Ryan Dahl's blog post acknowledging criticisms and adoption growth is publicly documented. The 404 error on deno.com and recent layoffs are plausible but not independently confirmed by major news outlets as of the knowledge cutoff. The article's characterization of user interest and product issues aligns with some community feedback but is largely anecdotal and speculative. Speculation about AI pivots and acquisitions lacks evidence. Overall, the article mixes factual information with opinion and speculation without citing multiple independent sources.
considerations: The article does not provide official statements from Deno or Ryan Dahl regarding layoffs or company status.
No independent verification of the website 404 error or the extent of layoffs is provided.
User adoption metrics and developer interest are discussed without quantitative data or third-party analysis.
The article reflects the author's personal views and frustrations, which may bias the narrative.
Potential positive developments or strategic plans from Deno are not explored in detail.
The impact of broader market conditions on Deno's challenges is not considered.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the challenges faced by Deno as a company and project, with mixed opinions on its leadership, technical decisions, and future viability. Some commenters defend Ryan Dahl and the contributions of Deno, while others criticize the project's execution, business model, and timing.

comments.author: dgreensp
comments.text:
I find the irreverent tone refreshing, personally.

As a founder who built all my prototypes and side projects on Deno for two years, I personally think Deno’s execution was just horrible, and avoidably so. Head-scratchingly, bafflingly bad decision-making.

I was the first engineering hire at Meteor (2012-2016), and we made the mistake of thinking we could reinvent the whole app development ecosystem, and make money at it, so I have the benefit of that experience, but it is not really rocket science or some insight that I wouldn’t expect Ryan Dahl and team to have, in the 2020s.

They were stretched thin with too many projects, which they were always neglecting or rewriting, without a solid business case. They coupled together runtime, framework, linting, docs, hosting, and packaging, with almost all of these components being inferior to the usual tools. The package system became an absolute nightmare.

If the goal was to eventually replace Node and NPM with something where TypeScript was first-class, there was better security, etc, they could have done a classic “embrace and extend.”
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed critique based on experience, highlighting specific technical and strategic missteps that align with common challenges in such projects.

comments.author: zoogeny
comments.text:
I have always wanted Deno to succeed. But it just seems to be too full of contradictions.

Their initial baffling stance about package.json was the first bad sign. I almost can't imagine the hubris of expecting devs to abandon such a large eco-system of packages by not striving for 100% support out of the gate. Of course they had to relent, but honestly the damage was done. They chose ideology over practicality and that doesn't bode well with devs.

I think they saw Rust and thought that devs were willing to abandon C++ for a language that was more modern and secure. By touting these same benefits perhaps they were hoping for similar sentiment from the JavaScript community.

Deno has some really good ideas (e.g. the library KV interface). I agree with a lot (but not all) of Dahl's vision. But the whole thing is just a bit too quirky for me to invest anything critical into an ecosystem that is one funding round away from disappearing completely.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately identifies a key early misstep that likely affected adoption and trust, reflecting a common issue in platform development balancing innovation and compatibility.

comments.author: gkoberger
comments.text:
I didn’t like the tone of this. Building a company is hard. Building an VC-backed open source product is really, really hard.

I know on HN we don’t always love CEOs, and that’s okay… the ethos of startups has changed over the past 10 years, and tech has shifted away from tinkerers and more toward Wall Street. But Ryan Dahl isn’t doing that; he’s a tinkerer and a builder.

I dunno, I just don’t like this vibe of “what have you done for me recently” in this post, especially given he skipped over the company and is calling out Ryan directly for some reason. Ryan is responsible for many of our careers; Node is the first language I really felt at home with.

Comparing him to Nero is gross.
comments.analysis: This comment defends the CEO and founder on a personal and professional level, emphasizing the difficulty of the task and the positive impact of his past work.

comments.author: skrebbel
comments.text: Seriously, imagine running a company like Deno, giving away most of your work for free, lots of shit not going right at all, gotta make hard decisions, fire good people etc, only to find some asshole call you out personally for... for not writing a blog post fast enough? The entitlement is through the roof here.
comments.analysis: Highlights the challenges and pressures on open source company leadership, criticizing entitlement from the community, which is a valid perspective though somewhat emotional.

comments.author: babaganoosh89
comments.text: Truth of the matter is Ryan Dahl is a suboptimal CEO. Being able to build good open source software does not have a strong correlation with being able to build a successful business.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly distinguishes between technical and business leadership skills, a common issue in tech startups, though it lacks nuance about the specific context.

comments.author: mattvr
comments.text:
Deno Deploy is actually an excellent product.

My choice ranking is Deno Deploy > Fly.io > AWS for new projects, depending on complexity and needs. They also have a new Deno sandbox feature which is great for running untrusted code, AI agents, etc.

The real question is can they adapt to customer feedback fast enough, focus priorities, adequately market & grow, make it profitable, etc. Bumpy road but definitely not doomed.

[0] https://deno.com/deploy
comments.analysis: Offers a balanced view acknowledging product strengths while realistically questioning business execution and adaptability.

comments.author: IshKebab
comments.text:
I really hope this is premature because Deno is easily the best thing to happen to the JS/TS ecosystem in years.

I agree with all the comments saying this is unnecessarily critical. We're getting an amazing tool totally for free. Quit complaining.

I would not be surprised if they get bought by one of the big AI players anyway, given the weird purchases of Bun and Astral.
comments.analysis: Expresses strong positive sentiment about Deno’s impact, which is valid but somewhat dismissive of legitimate criticisms.

comments.author: hardwaregeek
comments.text: I'm not fully convinced that there's a tenable model for open source devtool companies. Usually there's some handwavy plan to do hosting or code quality that never comes to fruition. Hosting is a hard business and the 800 pound gorilla in the room of AWS is even harder to surmount. Otherwise, I'm not sure what business model you can look towards. Support maybe?
comments.analysis: Insightful comment on the broader challenge of monetizing open source developer tools, which is a well-known industry problem.

comments.author: hmokiguess
comments.text: I could get behind some of this hate directed to Vercel’s CEO or even Cursor’s, but Deno is sort of like a breath of fresh air around the myriad of parasitic tech out there. Still, why so much hate? Who hurt you? What’s going on
comments.analysis: Defends Deno’s ethos and questions the negativity, reflecting emotional support but lacking detailed analysis.

comments.author: ashwinnair99
comments.text: Deno always felt like something built for the right reasons but at the wrong time. Good tech losing isn't new, it's just always a bit sad when it happens slowly.
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced view on timing and market conditions affecting technology adoption, a common theme in tech failures.
